hyperedges.label: Four Storage Algebras on a Shared Transactional Substrate

nodes.label: One Put per Key per Transaction
nodes.norm_label: one put per key per transaction

nodes.label: PostgreSQL JSONB Backend
nodes.rationale: PostgreSQL supplies the narrow versioning, deduplication, lease, and ACID capabilities needed by local Midnight clients without a heavyweight MongoDB service or ORM.

nodes.label: OpenSpec Sprint Process
nodes.rationale: Each module is specified and reviewed independently before implementation so requirements, design, tasks, and EARS scenarios remain auditable.

nodes.label: Single-Writer, Non-Distributed Scope
nodes.rationale: UmbraDB deliberately targets one writer and one Postgres instance; distributed trust and a Merkle layer are deferred beyond 1.0.0.

nodes.label: One Write per Key per Transaction
nodes.rationale: Rejecting same-key reuse makes timestamp and version addressing well-defined instead of silently losing an intermediate history row.
nodes.norm_label: one write per key per transaction

nodes.label: Checkpoint Manifest-Chunks Junction Table
nodes.rationale: A btree-indexed junction table replaces an array/GIN design that could not accelerate the scalar membership anti-join used by garbage collection.

nodes.label: Full VersionedEntry Property Comparison
nodes.rationale: Property P4 compares the complete versioned record so equal adjacent values cannot hide a broken version or timestamp mapping.

nodes.label: MongoDB Dependency and Nix Build Problem
nodes.rationale: MongoDB is compiled from source in the target Nix environment, adds a second service and database technology, and supplies capabilities this small custom store does not need.
nodes.norm_label: mongodb dependency and nix build problem

nodes.label: FerretDB Compatibility-Shim Rejection
nodes.rationale: FerretDB was rejected because its immature transaction and large-blob semantics are weakest in the exact multi-document checkpoint patterns UmbraDB needs.

nodes.label: Tier-1-Only Scope
nodes.rationale: The rebuild changes client persistence only; the separate Tier-2 indexer and TimescaleDB design remains unchanged.

nodes.label: Per-Sprint OpenSpec Process
nodes.rationale: The stale monorepo-era checklist is replaced by module-specific reviewed changes so implementation follows the corrected standalone-repository design.

nodes.label: Downstream Cutover Ownership
nodes.rationale: Rewiring and Mongo removal belong to the consuming midnight-dev-env repository rather than UmbraDB’s own task list.

nodes.label: One Postgres Instance, Two Schemas
nodes.rationale: Tier 1 and Tier 2 share an engine but remain isolated schemas to avoid collisions and accidental coupling.

nodes.label: FerretDB Rejection
nodes.rationale: A direct rewrite is safer than a compatibility layer because there is no production data and the shim lacks mature multi-document semantics.

nodes.label: Transaction-ID Reuse Guard
nodes.rationale: txid_current() detects a second write in the same transaction independently of wall-clock timestamp behavior.

nodes.label: Millisecond Timestamp Alignment
nodes.rationale: Database timestamps are truncated to JavaScript Date precision so a returned writtenAt can be used by getAt without falling just before the stored interval.

nodes.label: Temporal History Retention
nodes.rationale: A rolling pg_cron window bounds unbounded history growth, accepting a corresponding limit on how far back getAt can query.

nodes.label: Atomic Deduplication with GC-Clock Refresh
nodes.rationale: ON CONFLICT refreshes created_at so re-referenced old chunks remain protected from a concurrent grace-window GC pass.

nodes.label: Native ACID Transaction Layer
nodes.rationale: Standalone Postgres transactions remove MongoDB’s replica-set capability-detection branch.

nodes.label: Connection-Pinned Advisory Writer Lease
nodes.rationale: A session advisory lock must use one sql.reserve()-pinned connection for acquisition, holding, and release to prevent leaked locks across pooled connections.

nodes.label: Advisory-Lock Class Namespaces
nodes.rationale: Separate integer classes keep migration locks and writer leases from accidentally colliding on equal hashes.

nodes.label: Crash-Implies-Release Lease Semantics
nodes.rationale: Session locks release when the connection dies, intentionally trading Mongo’s durable TTL/fencing record for simpler single-process recovery.

nodes.label: Application-Encrypted bytea Storage
nodes.rationale: Opaque AES-256-GCM ciphertext is stored as bytea because JSONB queryability is useless for ciphertext and would add binary encoding overhead.

nodes.label: Conditional Legacy wallet_state Table
nodes.rationale: The table should exist only if a live consumer still uses the legacy store; production uses the checkpoint adapter instead.

nodes.label: postgres.js Driver
nodes.rationale: postgres.js preserves the project’s hand-written, parameterized SQL style without introducing an ORM.

nodes.label: Testcontainers Postgres Tests
nodes.rationale: Real Postgres is preferred over pg-mem because JSONB, advisory locks, ranges, and TOAST require engine-faithful behavior.

nodes.label: Unified Storage Interface Family
nodes.rationale: Four domain-specific modules share one error, async, validation, naming, and transaction-composition model.

nodes.label: Typed Thrown-Error Idiom
nodes.rationale: A single thrown-error idiom removes the cognitive seam between result unions in the lease layer and exceptions in other modules.

nodes.label: Lookup/Load/Try Absence Naming
nodes.rationale: Method names signal whether absence returns null, represents routine contention, or throws a not-found error.

nodes.label: Canonical TransactionHandle
nodes.rationale: One opaque handle lets modules participate in the same atomic unit without leaking backend-specific types.

nodes.label: Zod-First Boundary Validation
nodes.rationale: Schemas are the runtime source of truth for serializable data while live capability handles remain compile-time only.

nodes.label: tryAcquireLease
nodes.rationale: A nullable non-throwing companion keeps routine contention cheap without reintroducing a second error-handling idiom.

nodes.label: Global Chunk Deduplication and GC
nodes.rationale: Chunks are shared across wallets, so reclamation must test references globally even though checkpoint pruning is wallet-scoped.

nodes.label: PrivateStateProvider Direct-Table Implementation
nodes.rationale: The SDK interface has stateful scoping, key rotation, migration, and import/export semantics that do not fit TemporalKV’s simple generic contract.

nodes.label: Wallet State via CheckpointStore and Watermarks
nodes.norm_label: wallet state via checkpointstore and watermarks

nodes.label: TypeDoc Contract Validation
nodes.rationale: TypeDoc can validate cross-file TSDoc links and undocumented exports in CI without adding a second API schema language.

nodes.label: Standalone Repository Boundary
nodes.rationale: Sprint 1 provisions UmbraDB itself; integration and cutover remain responsibilities of the consuming repository.

nodes.label: getAt Replay-Equivalence Risk
nodes.rationale: Point-in-time correctness requires comparison with an independent replay oracle, not confidence that the SQL merely looks plausible.

nodes.label: Configurable umbradb Schema
nodes.rationale: The standalone library defaults to its own schema name while allowing hosts to override it.

nodes.label: TypeScript Migration Functions
nodes.rationale: Runtime schema identifiers cannot be bound as SQL values, so migrations use safe identifier interpolation in TypeScript.

nodes.label: btree_gist Migration Search Path
nodes.rationale: The migration connection temporarily includes public so a preinstalled extension’s operator classes resolve while all created objects remain schema-qualified.

nodes.label: Sprint 1 Retention Deferral
nodes.rationale: Sprint 1 keeps all history and explicitly postpones retention so correctness is established before pruning semantics are introduced.

nodes.label: Postgres BigInt Decoder
nodes.rationale: Configuring postgres.BigInt makes VersionedEntry.version satisfy its bigint runtime schema without string conversion or precision loss.

nodes.label: Three expectedVersion Put Paths
nodes.rationale: Absent, create-only, and positive-CAS cases need distinct SQL statement shapes so a missing row cannot be silently inserted during a guarded update.

nodes.label: Single-Snapshot getAt Query
nodes.rationale: Combining history and current branches in one statement prevents a concurrent write from splitting the read across inconsistent Read Committed snapshots.

nodes.label: Literal Prefix Matching
nodes.rationale: Percent, underscore, and backslash must be escaped or avoided as LIKE syntax so prefixes are interpreted literally.

nodes.label: Fail-Loud Transaction Participation Deferral
nodes.rationale: Until the transaction layer is wired, supplied handles are rejected explicitly so operations never escape an intended transaction silently.

nodes.label: External Claim Verification Rule
nodes.rationale: Every dependency or API claim must be checked against installed types or version-pinned upstream sources because prior reviews found plausible but incorrect assumptions.

nodes.norm_label: lean nat / postgresql bigint range obligation
